Name: info_theoretic_quantization
Title: Information-Theoretic Bounds for Model Compression and Quantization
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that information-theoretic bounds can be used to derive optimal quantization schemes for deep learning models, achieving a better trade-off between model accuracy and compression ratio.
Related Work: Recent works such as EMQ, SNIP, and AMP-ViT have explored mixed-precision quantization and adaptive post-training quantization methods. However, these approaches lack a rigorous information-theoretic foundation. Our proposal aims to fill this gap by establishing a theoretical framework for quantization based on rate-distortion theory and information-theoretic bounds.
Abstract: This proposal explores the application of information-theoretic bounds to model compression and quantization in deep learning. We aim to derive optimal quantization schemes that achieve a better trade-off between model accuracy and compression ratio. Our approach is based on rate-distortion theory and information-theoretic bounds, which provide a rigorous framework for analyzing the fundamental limits of quantization. We will investigate the use of entropy estimators, mutual information bounds, and Fisher information sensitivities to allocate bits efficiently and minimize task-relevant distortion. Our goal is to establish a theoretical foundation for quantization and compression in deep learning, with potential applications in efficient model deployment and knowledge distillation.
Experiments: Derive information-theoretic bounds for quantization using rate-distortion theory
Investigate the use of entropy estimators and mutual information bounds for bit allocation
Evaluate the performance of our approach on benchmark datasets and models
Compare our results with state-of-the-art quantization and compression methods
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of deriving information-theoretic bounds for large-scale deep learning models
The potential difficulty in estimating entropy and mutual information accurately
The need for careful tuning of hyperparameters and bit allocation strategies
